region_of_origin: Global; widely consumed across Asia, Africa, and the Middle East

ontology.foodex2_description: Seeds and seed products — watermelon seed (Citrullus lanatus)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for dried watermelon seed specifically; A00LC (seeds and seed products) is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dried watermelon seed as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried watermelon seeds is approximately 30 g (one small handful), providing around 167 kcal, 8.5 g protein, and 14.2 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Dried Watermelon Seed — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Dried Watermelon Seed (cr_id F09030029). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Fatty acid sub-fractions (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated) and sugar are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for watermelon seed kernels (NDB 12174) as HPB does not publish these sub-fractions for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — seeds and nuts category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dried watermelon seed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with other whole seeds and nuts (pumpkin seeds, sunflower seeds) which consistently fall in the GI range 10–35 in peer-reviewed tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The high protein (28.3 g), high fat (47.4 g), and low available carbohydrate (15.3 g, of which ~0.5 g sugar and ~3.5 g fibre) profile strongly supports a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (25 × 11.8 g available carbohydrate per 100 g × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 1.2 g, confirming negligible glycemic impact per serving. The input record's claimed GI of 76 (high) from an 'Embedded GI Database' is not an approved source and has been rejected; the analogous low-GI estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework is used instead.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (99 mg/100 g) with high potassium (648 mg/100 g) yields a favourable Na:K ratio supporting blood pressure regulation.
hypertension.guidance: Unsalted dried watermelon seeds are suitable for individuals with hypertension; choose unsalted varieties, as commercially salted products may contain 300–600 mg sodium/100 g, and limit portion to 30 g per serving to manage caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate (~11.8 g/100 g net), negligible sugar (~0.5 g/100 g), high protein (28.3 g/100 g), and high PUFA/MUFA fat profile collectively slow gastric emptying and support stable postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried watermelon seeds have a low estimated glycemic index (~25) and a glycemic load of approximately 1.2 per 30 g serving, making them a suitable snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a 30 g portion; avoid sugar-coated or flavoured commercial varieties.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dominant PUFA (27.6 g/100 g) and MUFA (9.4 g/100 g) with moderate saturated fat (10.0 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol support a cardioprotective lipid profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fat content of dried watermelon seeds supports cardiovascular health; limit to a 30 g serving to manage total caloric and saturated fat intake, and select unsalted varieties to avoid excess sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: PUFA/MUFA dominance (37 g/100 g) and micronutrients (magnesium, zinc, iron) support immune regulation, but saturated fat at 10 g/100 g may contribute to pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Unsalted, roasted dried watermelon seeds may be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given their dominant anti-inflammatory fat profile; avoid deep-fried or salted preparations that increase sodium and saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried watermelon seeds (Citrullus lanatus) are the dried and often lightly roasted seeds of the watermelon fruit, widely consumed as a snack across Asia, Africa, and the Middle East. In Singapore and Chinese communities, they are known as 西瓜子 (xi gua zi) and are a traditional Chinese New Year snack. The seeds are black in colour with a firm, crunchy shell and a mild, nutty-flavoured kernel inside. They are minimally processed — typically sun-dried or oven-roasted — and are sold plain or lightly salted. Nutritionally, dried watermelon seeds are energy-dense at 557 kcal per 100 g, with an exceptional protein content of 28.3 g/100 g and a high total fat of 47.4 g/100 g dominated by polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA ~27.6 g) and monounsaturated fatty acids (MUFA ~9.4 g), making them a plant-based source of heart-healthy fats. Carbohydrate content is low at 15.3 g/100 g with negligible sugar (~0.5 g) and useful dietary fibre (~3.5 g). Sodium is moderate at 99 mg/100 g for unsalted varieties, while potassium is high at 648 mg/100 g. Iron content is notable at 7.28 mg/100 g. The estimated glycemic index is low (~25), and the glycemic load per 30 g serving is approximately 1.2, making them suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, they are appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when consumed in a 30 g portion and in unsalted form. They are fully plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and halal.
common_user_queries: Is xi gua zi good for blood pressure?
Are watermelon seeds high in protein?
Can I eat watermelon seeds with hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09030029) at confidence 0.95. Fatty acid sub-fractions and sugar estimated from USDA FoodData Central watermelon seed kernels entry (NDB 12174) as a documented adaptation. GI estimated by analogy with whole seeds from University of Sydney GI Database framework. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Fatty acid sub-fractions (saturated_fat_g 10.0, monounsaturated_fat_g 9.4, polyunsaturated_fat_g 27.6) and sugar_g (0.5) and dietary_fibre_g (3.5) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12174 (watermelon seed kernels, dried) as HPB does not publish these sub-fractions for this item. This is documented as an adapted match. 2. The input record's claimed GI of 76 (high) from 'Embedded GI Database' has been rejected as this is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2. A low GI of ~25 is estimated by analogy with whole seeds and nuts from the University of Sydney GI Database framework (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). 3. Fields set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed vitamin, mineral sub-fraction, and moisture data for dried watermelon seed are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific entry; USDA FoodData Central NDB 12174 provides partial data for some micronutrients but values were not carried over to avoid mixing source confidence levels without explicit reconciliation. 4. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as watermelon seeds are a whole plant food with no industrial trans fat; this is confirmed zero by product formulation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source — naturally zero for a plant-based seed. 5. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible parent node for seeds and seed products; no dedicated FoodEx2 terminal code for dried watermelon seed was identified. 6. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (西瓜子), Malay (Biji tembikai kering), Tamil (உலர்ந்த தர்பூசணி விதை), and Hindi (सूखे तरबूज के बीज) reflecting the food's broad consumption across Singapore's multicultural communities.
